speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Years ago, she remembered some of the early residents and businesses in Viola, the Four Mile Church, literaries and debates. Her father fared well in 1893, but times were hard and she helped raise her siblings, work in the garden in Cannes. She recalls Alice Skeems' bout with smallpox and her own case of typhoid fever. In 1912, she left the area and homesteaded with her husband in Canada.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Hello.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Is it on?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it should be working, yeah.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Should I start it now?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Sure. Well, a man named Asher Palmer was the first man I think was there and he had two sons. Wesley and Elmer. Wesley was a, he was the oldest and he had, he was justice of the peace for a long time and also he raised the first apples in that country, first apples there. And then this man named John Miller homesteaded there. His homestead ran back of Viola, you know, but he lived in Viola. And so did Elmer Palmer lived in Viola, but the homestead run back, you see. And it's there, yeah, they still are. And Bob Bowles had a store in the post office. And I forgot the man's name, it had the blacksmith shop. And we went to church every Sunday in a schoolhouse. And my father, Frank Hill, taught the first term of school there. Mr. Nichols, an old man he was, carried the mail in a covered wagon from Viola to Palouse and back.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were you doing that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah. To Palouse and back.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was it a big wagon that he had?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Just a little covered light wagon he had. He carried the mail there and back for years and years. And then a man named McCray had a saloon there, of course. He got that saloon, you know. Later, Mr. Manson had a box factory after they got to raising apples and he had a box factory there. And Mr. and Mrs. Comer, they had the hotel there. And they later built a church, one room. Mr. Schmiley was the school superintendent and Mr. Beach was the minister. Now that's as far as I know of that. But this church, it got burnt down. I don't know, they burnt both schoolhouses. The boy didn't wanna go to school. And he burnt both schoolhouses. And then they fixed it up in an old hall for him to have some school. And he burnt the hall. Then they fixed up the church and he burnt the church so that they had, he didn't wanna go to school. And that's what happened. And just a boy about 12 years old. But we all thought there was somebody else helping him. Now that's all I know of that.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: So that's the beginning of that big town. That's the beginning of that town.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When you say the Comer, did the Comers build the church?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, Comer had the hotel. No, the members built the church. A church member, Christian church. Of course, it's small. There wasn't too many people to go. It was small.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Where did they have church before that? Did people go to church at all?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They had in the schoolhouse, in the schoolhouse.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would someone come out and preach?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes. Yes, before we got the church, they came out from Cluse and preached. But after we got the church, we had a minister there. Just come out, would stay there and preach. Oh, he worked some on the farms like that too. That he preached.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How'd they raise the money for that church? Do you know how they got that going?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Sometimes, you know. It didn't take much to run a church then as you do now. Goodness sakes. They didn't dress that like they do now. Oh, didn't. And the minister, he didn't get nothing much. Just what the congregation would take up, you know, in the congregation and give him. That's all he'd give. Maybe five, six dollars on Sunday, that's all. Now look what they're giving. And a calico dress. You can get a calico dress. 25 cents. Good dress. You get five yards, five cents a yard.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: We get dressed in apron both for that. 36 inches wide.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I guess those people are all gone now, I guess. That's it, that's it. I'm sure.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: So that's about all I know about Viola.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was there a grist mill there when you were in Viola? A grist mill?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah. No, never was. Wasn't in school city, but not in Viola. They saw mills back in the timber. No grist mill, no.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: No, they never did have one there, did they? Well, there's a story about, do you know how John Rothwell lost his leg? Oh, how did that happen? With his leg?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: It seems to me like they had these old horse powers. The horses would go around, there was a tumbling rod that'd run from the machine up to this horse power. And somebody got caught in that tumbling rod, and they'd have to step over it or something and caught his leg in that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What were they using the horse power for? Oh, I don't know, what was they using?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And then there's a Beasley girl got hurt there too, later on and like that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what was they doing?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They was grinding grain or something. I just can't remember that. Grinding grain or something though. She had to keep the horses going and step over that rod and she didn't get over once. Oh, it'd have accidents sometimes. Oh, you know, there's so many people around, they have to have accidents.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What was that box factory about? In Bali?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, the... It was down in the south end. Yeah, it was down in the south end. After a long time. I don't know what Bali looks like now, I haven't seen it for a long time. Father lived in Washington, but his fence, his fence, yeah, is the line between Idaho and Washington. It still is. Because we used to play back and forth, one getting out, the other one getting...

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: That's about all. There was lots of strawberries around, around in the east of Bali there, in the mountains. There was lots of strawberries there and cattle.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you go out there and pick strawberries?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yes, we went out and picked strawberries. I see.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: You know, I wanted you to go back to, you started to mention last time, right before I left, some stuff about your parents, things that they told you. I think it was your parents coming by covered wagon.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean my fault?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah. Oh yeah, they come from Oregon, Junction City, Oregon. Took them six weeks to come because they stopped and worked a while on the way. And when they got to the homestead, all they had to eat was, Mother said they had a cup of tea and some bread and butter and an onion. That's all they had when they got to their homestead. You know, but they had the money, but they just didn't bother the grocers because they didn't get things. Then there's Mr. Scott lived there close. He had a big garden and a Mr. Platts. They helped the folks out then. Folks got there in July, then they helped the folks out in the garden stuff all that summer. So they got along good then. But Father loaned the only $25 they had to Mr. Scott. Mother said he didn't have any money, so all he did, he loaned it out. He was like that. He'd give you the last cent he had and do that himself. And he'd help somebody else. Well, anyway, Mother said he didn't like it, but he'd give it back. I tell you, that's all right.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How would they do those first years? Was that the thing?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Father taught school in that fall. They had to build a log house and the neighbors have to haul out the logs and build a log house. And then Father taught school at fall in Viola there. And they got along pretty good that way. Oh, they didn't have it like we do nowadays, goodness knows, but then they raised the garden after the next summer and they got along all right after that. Father taught school for several years after he was married.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I don't know, people's all the same. There wasn't one trying to above another and not trying to get the money away from, there wasn't, didn't anybody have much money. But they were good friends, you know, and all on the same. All had a good time. And they had neighbors.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember the homestead being like when you were a kid?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, just a big bunch of grass, about two feet high all over. I remember Father used to take the three horses, put it on a plow, and my brother and I would go along behind him and he'd plow up what they call camers. Your little onion, like something like an onion, well it wasn't strong. And we'd eat that, the Indians eat it. Father told us about it. Indians eating camers so we eat it too. And then once in a while we'd catch a rabbit, little baby rabbit, you know, take it home. Oh yeah, since the kind of rough, near as I remember, it's a rough looking country, just starting into farming, all bunch of grass all over and only three little horses. And about a 12 inch plow to go around and walk, round and round and round.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Now were you born at Fiola?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well I was born there, not in the, not in the homestead. Yeah, about two miles southwest of Fiola. I told you, you look back and you get up there on the mountain, you can see that house. You can't see the house because of trees. But you can see an old red barn and a lot of trees. But the old house is there but it's pretty well done now. They built it when I was 12 years old. That's a long time ago you see.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did they have help on that when they put that in? The new house?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, they got help on it. They never had that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: No, did they have help when they built that house?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, they had a new one. A carpenter. Oh yes, yeah. Then they finally got telephones put in Fiola there. We lived in Fiola after we was married and then they put telephones in there too. I don't think, we never had electricity though. Nobody did around there. Well I guess they have now, I'm sure they have.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: So that's a long time ago.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: When you're 100 years ago, I guess, since Fiola was started. Because I was four years old. No, they were there four years before we, my brother and I were born, we were twins. And they were there four years before we were born. So you see there, they were there right from the beginning of Fiola. I think there was a man there. No man named Smiley was there, about the first I guess. He was a kind of retired, a kind of a minister.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Then they had church, we had a good big church later on. We had a church go. And I had a good church. She went to Sunday School there, her and her sister.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You can remember at Kinsley -Bellum, that church? How much?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I remember the church. I don't know what it looked like. They went down and they built another church and they've used it for a shop now. They got a, oh I don't know what got into them. They, the Nazarenes, they had a church there, you know. And they met this Eddie Gray's father. He put a furnace in, fixed it all up and was having church in there. I don't know whether it was a matter of the Christian people that they said they owned the land and they wasn't gonna have no Nazarenes in there so they had to quit. So Eddie took his furnace out. Not Eddie, but Eddie's. Never had nothing since then. So that turned into a shop. Some kind of a shop, I don't know. Though that makes it bad.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't they leave it alone? Even though all the Christian people that owned the church, what's the difference?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They didn't need that. Do that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you think this good?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, no. That's the way things go like that sometimes. Yes, it is.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, that's about all I can tell you.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Well, can you tell me more about when you were a kid, you know, things that you did just, you know, for fun? What else, what? Things that you did for fun when you were a kid? When you had some free time?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah, we played up and down the creek in the water. And neighbor girls would come up, you know, and the neighbor kids and we'd all go to the creek, you know how kids are and what. We'd catch pollywugs and the boys would catch snakes.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Boys would start the Sunday school on a Sunday. If mother didn't go and father, sometimes they couldn't go. And then they'd send the boys and they'd go up the swimming hole. My brother would always get, my twin brother, he'd always get his shirt on wrong side out and come up. Mother didn't know they hadn't been to church.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, they'd see you in swimmer town till they seen Mr. Poes coming off in church and they'd get out and get their clothes on right quick. Well, Ace said, Olive's getting his shirt on wrong side out. See, that was a bad giveaway. Course I didn't bear to tell on them, they're with me.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I didn't, no?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: But we didn't go to school there much. We went down Four Mile School down west of Owely. We went to Four Mile School. When they first went there, the Four Mile School was too far away for us to go. So we went to Viola for a while. And then Miranda Hammers was the name of the teacher taught there. When we went, father would quit teaching then. He'd went to farming.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, they all had work. Nothing to work with much either. Didn't have tools then like they do now. Milk cows sell butter and eggs, sell eggs, and sell garden stuff. Us used to carry onions up. We'd go to school and we'd take onions up and sell them in Bauley there. Little bunches of green onions, you know. Things like that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What kind of jobs, chores did you kids have to do?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, milk cows, feed the pigs. The boys did. I had to help mother in the house. The boys had to do the chores. And then they got big enough. I always had to help take care of the kids and the baby, all those in the baby luck after. And then dishes to wash and everything. It was a lot of work to do. And had to work in the garden. Father couldn't do it and farm too. So us kids had to work in the garden. We didn't play around like they do now. We had to. They made us get out and then father just put up so many roles for us to do. And we'd have to do them or else take a licking. I don't think he'd hurt as much, but we scared of him just to say. Well, he had to because we just didn't, he just couldn't do all that much, you know. Mother happened in the garden a lot too. The garden was probably pretty big for all you kids. Yes, it was. Big garden. And you had to make better with that old and you had to wash your churn up and down, you know, like that. Bella done it, she knows that. See, mother'd sit down of an evening and churn and churn and churn on her old butter. She put it off on a cool evening and then she did the rest. She said she'd go on a rest and churn.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And they had to, oh, oh, we had ponies to ride. Oh yes, we'd go horseback riding and we'd go, on Sundays we'd go riding. Boys used to take the horse and run races on Sundays. If they could get it away from father, not let him find it out while he'd run races on Sundays. If father found that, he would let him have the horse.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah, that's what. That's what. Well.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you have your hands full taking care of the kids, the smaller kids?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, I had to always have to take care of the baby. Mother had so much to do. I was the oldest, the oldest girl. Mother had so much to do that she had to, I had to help. Well, I didn't know, I didn't mind. Well, all the older schools knew how you do. Oldest of the family, all you had to take care of it. You know, one or the other. Well, you had to work, you had to live. And I remember I was 12 years old before I ever had a pair of over boots. You know, wear over your shoes to school. No, we didn't, we had to just wear our shoes. Like our shoes was good and heavy too, I'll tell you that. We had no fine shoes. We wore them to school. And our old school house, it was just one room and a big old box heater, long. Then it had a hearth come out on it. It took this three foot wood and put it in that. And then, oh, you know, wood don't throw that heat out like coal or anything, but we didn't have any coal. And we'd drive down there with a team and we'd be cold and sometimes, oh, we'd push each other out in the snow and get wet and cold till we got there. Then that old heater wouldn't heat the house, it was very cold. I don't know how we lived through it, but we never got sick, it didn't hurt us. No. Get stuck in the snow drift and all of us have to get out and wade in that snow up to our waist, they have to get.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But now, could they do that nowadays?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I guess they could, they had to. Well, father told us, we thought we had to, he told us no, he says, you get stuck in the snow drift, you sit still and leave the horses alone. Says, they'll flounce through and get through and you stay in the sleighs and leave them alone. They'll get through and they're dead. We thought we had to get out and help, but it's too heavy for them. Heh heh heh.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, we,

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: father took his grain to the fluce and get it ground for flour. And then he raised beans on his summer follow -up, enough to raise the sugar and coffee, buy his sugar and coffee. Oh yes, he'd have two, three acres of beans in. And he'd have to hire help from Viola. The mama would come down from Viola and help hold it, you know, hold the beans out and help pull them and thrash them out. I tell you, we worked on them. If they couldn't thrash them out, then they'd put them in the barn and then they'd trash them in the wintertime when they had more time, you know, on the barn floor. Put a big canvas down and then they'd beat them out with frills, you know. You take a stick, you know what a frill is. Well, they'd take a stick and then they'd take a shorter one, you see, and they'd put a leather here on this and then up over this and then this. You take this and fly back, see, and then when you hit it that way, well, this stick on top would go down over the beans and that's what they call the frill. It is shorter than this one, the lower one. And you'd hit that, you hit it hard.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And then that - Did you do that? Did you do that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I didn't, the men folks did. Yeah, that's the way they had to do it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't do that nowadays, would they?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, that's a pretty hard way to get your beans. Oh, they had to work hard. Oh yes. Then they used to have headers in that country. They'd head, we'd get the headers and stack it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I think - Was that pretty early that they had headers?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes. Header bed was high on one side, low on the other because the shoot from the header would have to come up into this bed. And you see the grain had drapers. So they'd keep it rolling around like this. Well, this grain would get on these drapers and then they'd go and fall over in the header bed, see. And then this was low. And then it just built this like this, you know. Come down like this. And then this height would hold the grain in and this would let the shoot go up in here. And there'd be somebody there to keep it back, you know, to have it loaded. Then father got a binder and bound the grain and then they could get it taken care of quicker. 1993.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Let's see. That's that wet year. 19.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: It was 1993, wasn't it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They had that wet place. The wet - That was 1993. They had it. 1893, it rained. It rained and bought their crops. But father got his sake because he had the binder and he'd had it all bound and he had his trash all done. So he saved his crop that year. But most of the people that lost their grain rained so much. That was 1893, yes.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were most other people using headers then?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes. Well, nearly all of them did. I don't know how father had to get his binder, but he did. He got it some way. He saved his grain because they could cut it on the green side and then they'd shock it up in shocks and then it would mature, you see. Then they could go by and pick it up and put it in the machine. Trash it. But the header, you had to let it set quite a little while before you could trash it after you got it cut. It had to be just as ripe as it could be before they could cut it too. It was ready to shatter out before they, well, they did shatter a lot of it did.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: You were lucky then. Yes, oh yes, we were lucky. Father sold a lot of his grain for seed wheat that next spring.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: But they got groceries and stuff from the coast over on the coast, Seattle and around. They sent him groceries and stuff. I don't know, they traded that old wet wheat for something. I don't know, maybe pig feet or chicken feet or something. I don't know just what. Over on the coast they took it and let us have so much, let people have groceries.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did many people have to leave or sell their land because of that bad harvest?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I can't remember of anybody leaving. No, they made it through. That's the only time. It was just a wet.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They often laid real good gardens down there. Didn't rain quite often. Raised good in the garden. Didn't have to irrigate anything.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You still got the water on back there?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I forgot that. Yes, sir. All wet, they didn't want that. Well, I forgot about that water.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Mother usually used it on about half an hour and said, cut that off on that button up. Yeah, but his thing's on. She don't know it's out there. I mean, it's good water and I did too. She'll take me.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me about how you used to can in the oldest way that you can remember?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, I remember the first speech your mother can, she had a half a gallon cans.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Tin cans?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: There were tin. They weren't high can, they were kind of, not flat, but you know, half gallons. And the lid, if I can make you understand, they'd come up and then this lid would, you see there'd be a little page come in, this lid would come over the top like this. There'd be a little crease around here, add edge. And then this lid from the inside would come out over the top, the peaches. And then mother would put red sealing wax all in this crease, clear around that can. All over that and make it airtight. That's what they had then.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would they boil it then after they did that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, no, no mother always cooked her peaches first. You'd cook them and you'd put them in the tin and then you'd just seal it. Real hot. Just take them right out and seal them real hot. The sealing wax then you see would go all over that and then it would cool them and then seal. Yeah, the tin cans, I don't know. Now they won't use tin. And everything that way. They didn't, I don't want to make sure they can in the garden stuff. They put it in a cellar. They'd put cabbage. Father used to make a kind of a frame out in the garden. He'd put up a piece, he'd put a piece across like this and down here, oh it'd be long. Oh, according to how long you want it. And then he'd tie two heads together. He'd dig a trench about half a foot deep, six inches deep. Put some straw in there and they'd tie these cabbage roots together. Let the head lay down in the straw and then he'd put more straw over the heads until he got that whole thing full and then he'd put some dirt over them, cover them over so they wouldn't freeze. That's the way he had cabbage all winter. That way. I don't.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did he put a lot of dirt on top?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, he had to. It was freezing, he did. He got off a horse, you know, then. He put quite a bit of straw until the dirt couldn't get next to cabbage, you know. He put quite a bit, there'd been none until dirt. Oh, he'd put it all over. Then he'd have to go in the one end and get the cabbage out. Then they used to bury their potatoes. They'd dig down about maybe a couple feet down in and then they'd put their potatoes in pits, you know. And then he'd put straw. He'd pile them up and they'd come up like, yes, see, they'd come up like this. And then he'd put straw all over them and maybe put it in half two feet deep and then throw dirt on top of that, quite a lot of dirt. And they'd keep all over there. Oh, that was a job in the spring, getting them out of there. Well, I hated that, wasn't anything, we're not getting them potatoes out of the pits. I hate to say, father, go and open one of them and say, now when that dries, I want you kids to put those potatoes all in the bags. We had them.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What was so bad about doing that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, the kid didn't want to work. It wasn't bad, we just didn't want to do it. We'll tell you, you know, you keep it from the bottom up even and you know, it wouldn't be so bad.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: First they'd give them a shove and they'd hear that come. I'd have mine all clean so I could pick them up from here, you know, and they'd shove that all through them here. I'd have to get back and pick them up. You know how the boys fly. I thought there was a million stupid boys in the world. I thought there was a million stupid boys in the world. It made rational scuffle play. So I'd get mine done and I'd have so many to pick up and I'd get mine done and they'd say, well, if you'll help me, I'll pay you. I'll give you 10 cents. I knew they wouldn't. They never had 10 cents. I knew they wouldn't but yet I'd help them. I couldn't, I said, well, that's one thing it hurt me to have them pick up. They wouldn't help me though. Oh, they were on right. That's a little bee.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes. Awful cold in the wintertime there though. Thought he used to wrap up. He didn't have anything much to wrap up in so he'd take gunny sacks, cut them in pieces and then start getting. He had kind of a heavy boots on and he started to round his feet and he'd wrap clear up to his hips because he's in that snow cutting wood, just bringing that log of wood out of timber for wood. And he'd come back just soaking wet. Just being wet from that snow. And then he'd hang them. I remember he had a place up in the house. He'd hang them old sacks up and let them dry in the night and then put them on. Yep, next morning. Go get another load of wood.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz

But there wouldn't be now because there's so many people farming and houses, people living back in there. There's no timber. Not like it used to be. I know they used to have to go over to Summit to get their wood before we was married alone. They'd have to go take their team, go back over to Plantingham Creek and up in there and get their wood. I say people had settled up so far back in back of Viole there, you know, and had homes in there. And they would let them have the wood. Oh, some of them might let them come along now and then, but at first there's lots of wood there.

And then the mother would can the fruit, but they didn't can beans or peas or nothing like that. Those days, they just didn't use them. They have all kinds of carrots and cabbage and potatoes. And they'd raise lots of dry beans. You know, I've had lots of dry beans.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz

He'd get five of them, hold five and six cents a pound for them on that mountain point beach, you know.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, we didn't have cans of them. The vegetables, we had to have them through, peaches and pears. We had lots of apples. After they got the raisin apples. But they didn't think if he could raise apples for Wes Farmer, they'd sell them. The reason I remember that so well, I sold, I had a calf, who I claimed that the father said he wanted to buy it. So I told him all right, he could have it for a dollar. That was big money. Then mother took my dollar and bought apples for the harvest hands. So I can't go down. That's the reason I remember Wes Farmer raising apples.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: She took my dollar and raised that and bought apples. People thought they couldn't raise them, you know. They never put them out. They said, you know, you can't do it. No, it's too cold. No, he raised the nicest apples. Then everybody raised too many. He asked, apples ever. Well, he raised all kinds of fruit then, better than he can now. There's no bugs or things like this now. They raised old bugs, used to have nice pears, cherries, paper cuts, peaches, raspberries, gooseberries, currants, and two different kinds of currants, the black and the red and the white. Raspberries and oh, I don't know, anything like that, but they did hand them. Made jam. They didn't hand them.

They didn't live very good. And they couldn't raise a very good garden in the timber, you know, too much serpentine stuff in the soil, the timber and the trees. They couldn't raise too much garden there. I don't know how the people in the timber did live. My father used to get them out of hay, sell them hay, he'd never get nothing from us. speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz

Same way in Viola they did too. And church at four mile in Viola, they both.

We'd have after an hour, and then they'd have the church.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Yes, it was, we had one man, Amet Laddow, who was a minister, and boy, what's the equipment?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: That old church would just be packed. Mud, oh, we had to go through the mud to get there. It was 25 of us baptized in that four mile creek. One Sunday, they had to cut the ice, the thick ice off, and put it out on the way so they could baptize us. And that minister stood in that water, baptized 25 of us. My mother, I know mother had a quilt, and I was baptized, she had a quilt to put around me. We had to drive for a little ways, and had to get to a neighbor's place where they could let us come in and change our clothes before we'd go home. Never heard of this. We never got cold. We got cold, yeah, but I mean, never got no colds or anything. That water was icy. Oh, that old preacher, he stood there and he baptized all of them. He wasn't a very big man either. Some of them boys, he baptized us bigger than he was, taller. Put them under. It was cold. Then later on, he baptized a lot more, and the weather was better then. Charlie here was baptized, and one of my brothers, I guess both of my brothers was baptized. I know a lot of people, they come from all around. He had a good church, I mean. Was this still at the Viola Schoolhouse, or was this at the... That's a four mile school.

Well, the farmers kept him in the, and they'd keep him in the vegetables, and they'd give him a little money on Sunday, you know, to take up a collection. And then, a lot of them would give him meat, you know, everybody had their own meat. And they'd give his ministry, just him and his wife wouldn't take much. And they'd give him a little collection on Sunday. He got one for good. I remember Mother taking them, he lived in Blue City. I remember, I know Mother and Mrs. Poe, that's a neighbor. They took a hack, we had a light wagon, we called them hacks. And they took it in a whole lot, a whole back full of vegetables and so on. They put it in the center for the winter. So they got one for good.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yes, he'd go and he'd do in the spring of the year, or anytime, he'd go and help if he could. But living in town that way, it's about 10 miles of blues from our place. Just with a team, you know, he didn't have anything, any way to get out there. I'm one of the, the preacher and I, he was staying around with the members. Just stayed out there with the people. In the wintertime, you know, and everybody could come. Oh, he's coming from Kamiak and all around up there. Just that old, just big, oh. He's got a lot of joiners.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz

But we'd have, oh I don't know, we played games and different things like that. Then later on, they got some of the good dances, but Father never allowed us to dance, you know. I tell you, they got so rough and drinking so much around there that Father said, no, you can't go to dances, it's not fair. So we didn't, I never learned to dance. He wouldn't let us. He wouldn't let the boys go either. And he wouldn't let the boys play cards either. They never did play. I don't know, I guess because they got so old and didn't know how or what, I don't know. Because Father wouldn't stand for it. So we had to do this because, we never thought it'd make bad though.

Homemade freezer, or home freezers. We'd have to freeze it. Well, we'd all have to turn that. Till we got some hard, we couldn't turn it anymore. Then it was done. That was good ice cream though. We used to put the real cream in it, some eggs, fix it up real good. The rich.

words: We didn't have it though. We had ice and we put it in an underground cellar. And then we put a straw at the top and it was covered, oh that, it'd be covered that deep with dirt to see the frame. And the logs and I couldn't hold that up. And that all be covered in that big heavy door. We'd keep that there nearly all summer. I don't know why they didn't put ice up down there, but they didn't. They'd have to go tell them to get their ice. Oh, get their ice cream. Well up there in Canada, when we was up there, we went there in July. We had a pulley in the top. They pulled the water out of the well with a pulley. You know, the rope on the end and then the wheel. Go over this little wheel and then we'd pull it up this way. Well our well was, well we had a door, we wheeled it out as we get. And if you go there in July, let your bucket down and you can take something and knock the ice off the side of the well. Turn the bucket and bring it up and make your ice cream. That's in July. That's when they're, June or July is when their water pipes would freeze up. You see, it'd be nice and warm on top, but the water, the rocks went down. You see, if you go down and freeze their pipes and it'd be warm up here on top, you wouldn't hardly leave it. But you see it going down. And they put their pipes down 10 feet deep. Oh yes, that ground freezes up there. You can't, you couldn't get a bit of that ground up the other day. Not even with the, you'd have to pick it like a rock. Just solid. Well tell you what they did, anybody died, they'd have to put them in the cemetery in that little house there in the spring to bury them. They couldn't dig the grave. No, no, no, no. One time we had the flu there and that little house was just full of people just stacked up. They never got to bury them until the next spring. Of course it was, oh, white, it had the, oh what you call it, in the, clean the walls, the deep, wide walls, deep walls to keep it cool in there and help with the bodies. And they couldn't, no, they wouldn't dig it till the weather stopped and the ground thought out next spring. Oh, that's terrible. When everybody died and that stick in the little house froze up till spring. I couldn't understand the thoughts of that. But didn't really have a sky.

words: Yeah, I've seen him. The reason I... I'll say that or not, but... Mother used to say, well, I don't know that old lady, Palmer, she's that poor old man. He's up in his 90s and she'd say, well, Asher, you must go out now and hoe in that garden. And we was just about to take the garden. You'll have to go out and hoe. Mother thought that was terrible. That's the reason I remember his name was Asher. Mother said you'd have to go out and hoe in the garden. I thought how mean that was. Let that old man get out there.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: His wife would tell him to do that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, she wouldn't do it. She wouldn't go, she'd make him go. Think about making an old man like him go out and hoe in the garden. Gosh, he might fall down.

speaker: Velma Gertz
words: He'd go out the garden, wouldn't he?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: He'd do like he did last summer, go out there and fall down in the corn. He got out here the other day and he fell down. He couldn't get up a step in the back. And he howled and he howled and he fell and I didn't hear him, either one of us. The door was open too. He finally made it in the house.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: He says, didn't you folks hear me calling?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I said, no, couldn't hear you talk. He said, well, I'd like to never got in the house. I said, well, it's too bad you could sit out there.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I don't think you get a word with it. We didn't have the Taliban, we were just sitting there like this. The door was open.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, it's nothing wrong with him.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: An old lady here, up here in the home, she's 105, she got her first permanent.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And she can hear, she don't wear glasses. She can see good, only when she wears glasses. She don't get around very good. She can hear, her mind is clear as a bell. No, see, 105. That's good.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, we grew up together. We went to school together. Oh, I don't know. I can't, first I can remember, he was around ever since I can remember. We went to school together.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you court for a long time, or did you, is he the main, boy that you ever went out with anywhere?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, I went with others.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Got mad at him, told him to do it. I went with somebody else.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you get mad about him? Was just some little thing got you?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, he went and took another girl to a party, and I wanted to go and he left me at home. Took another girl and that made me mad. I told him he'd go and go and go and go right there. That's all he did to, all winter. I wouldn't go with him. All winter.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Would you?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: When they leave you at home, they knew you wanted to go and go get somebody else.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't that make you mad?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, well, I guess everybody has trouble somewhere, don't they?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you got married?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: 20, I was 20, 22 when Valmon was born.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you, when you got married, where did you move, where did you move to? Where'd I go to? Yeah, and where'd you, you know, where'd you set up your house?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, well, this was in back of West Farmers too there. There was a man had some grain there and his boy died and he couldn't handle all this grain then after his boy died, so he, this is in back of West Farmers, so we had to go back. We bought this out and we wasn't going to get married in that fall, and then when he bought this wheat, my brother, and he bought the wheat together, then we got married and moved up there in that house and stayed there that summer. Then we went out of place over toward Moscow and we stayed over there for a year.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And then what did we do?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh, we come back to Viola and Charlie worked with a, hauled out timbers for that big old, the railroad that went through from Spokane, that electric. Oh, inland. Inland. And they hauled out, him and my brother hauled out the timbers for that trestle. It was there, it wasn't there anymore. But it was, trestle was there. And they hauled out those big timbers for that. And Charlie worked on the railroad quite a lot. They had a big camp there, you know, Viola there. In back of Grace. I think that they lived there. It seemed like that they lived there. These, they had men living in Miller's house. That's where John Miller lived. And one of the boy's wives died while he was there with the PB. And they, they was working on the railroad, they puttin' that railroad through there then. They started working on that railroad then a long time before they got it finished. Oh yes. That used to be nice. I wonder why they ever quit using it. I don't know why they ever took the trestle out. Maybe it got so it wasn't safe or something. I don't know. That was handy. In the wintertime, you know, we'd just walk up there to the little depot they had there and go to Moscow and back. Come back home, there's no place to put a horse or anything. They could walk from the father's house all up there though a little bit. That was handy, nice to have that railroad through there and then they shipped wheat and stuff from, you know, they had the big elevators there. That was handy. I don't know why they couldn't use none. Maybe it wore out.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When you got married, did people give you things to help set you up at all?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, they never give us nothing. We never had anything, only what we... We didn't have anything, hardly. No showers or anything like that. But I tell you, we had $25. And I tell you what we did, we bought a cook stove for the wood stove, you know, to burn wood in. A good little stove, a good size stove. Didn't have any warmer on it, but it was just a stove. And they had a little water, a little tank on the back for put water in and then they had a hearth. And then we bought a kind of a Davenport and four chairs for $25. Of course, the Davenport had been used.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: The stove was new?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, it was new and the chairs were new. One rocking chair and for $25. And I sold the stove for what was it, $10 when I sold it. It's a good stove yet. I don't know what made me move.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I went to, oh, we went to Canada, no?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I don't know why I sold it. But I did. Oh, I know, Mother had a home comfort and she decided it wasn't big enough so she told me I could have it, the home comfort. So I took her stove and then I had mine to sell and she bought a bigger one.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, we, that seemed like a long time ago though. It was. It was in your century.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did Viola change much in the time that, well, didn't the Comer's hotel burn down?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, the hotel burned.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I can't remember. Some way from a cigarette in the bed or something.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Who used to stay there? Did many people stay there all by their?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, there was people stayed there. They were nice people and they had a little store in there, in their old notions, you know, in their hotel. Like candy hearts and old little notions like that, candies and dumbs and like young people want. But Bowles had the groceries and everything over across the street on his, yeah, that hotel burned. It was a nice little hotel too. Wasn't very big, but it's big enough for there. Oh, it was three stories or something. Yeah, it was two stories, two or three stories.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Hey, where did you all bother playing around with them? Were there tent meetings in Viola?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Oh yeah, I'll say there was. The Methodists used to hold camp meetings there, the big tents and boy would they shout. And then some of them old ladies, you know, get religion and would they shout. They were nice old people. And then they'd hold that there maybe two, three weeks at a time. And they'd camp around there and have good meetings, have meetings at night. And then they'd have meetings in the afternoon for those people that were living there, camp there, you know, but the farmers, I know we used to go at night quite a lot, not every night. We couldn't, but we would go. And they had lots of nice meetings, nice singing.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that different than the faith people? Do you remember when they came in? The who? Faith people?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, I don't know about them. Oh, these are Methodists. These are the Methodist people.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Methodist had camp meetings?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They were two old Christians anyway, they're old.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, they used to have good meetings there. I used to like to go and hear them. I thought it was funny, it said under 10th in the meeting, in camp meetings. So.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How come they didn't meet in the church?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: It wasn't big enough.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it wasn't big enough?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: It wasn't no church, no church, it was a schoolhouse. The schoolhouse. This was really early then. The schoolhouse wasn't very big. All right, I went to the schoolhouse one time, we used to have dinners, church dinners in the schoolhouse. Well, I remember us kids was outside and the women after dinner, they'd put the things away and they were sitting there talking in the schoolhouse and I come in and I seen that woman sitting there and I looked at her.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Mother, who's that old squaw sitting over there?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Shh, come here. Mother said, come here. You sit down there, she says, Mrs. Jill. I said, who's that old squaw, I wanna know. Sit down, sit down. She says, that's Mr. Po's sister. She says, sit down and then behave yourself. Shut up, shut up. Well, I had to find out who she was before I could. She looked like an Indian, I hated an Indian. See, they used to have Indians and one of them folks come up there, they had an Indian scare, everybody had, they even had a fort built for the Indians, they'd play the Indians and they had this fort where they could go get in there. Indians never did come but there's a way they might and they could all go to this fort. Well, I always thought to be afraid of Indians. I wanna know what she's doing there. I wouldn't go and see there, but these are.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you see Indians very much?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah. They were peaceful though up there. They didn't bother anybody. They used to go. I think they went from Nespelem up here over in there by Greensville and around.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it an Indian reservation or it used to be something?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, they'd go from Nespelem over to that reservation in the back, you could see them going along the road. You know how they string out, one behind the other and the dogs and then they'd have a wagon and all the women are sitting flat down in it. I used to be afraid to walk the vowel, if I'd seen an Indian. If I'd seen one, I think I'd have hidden a corner of the fence. They had a fence that went this way and this way. And I said, mother, if I see an Indian coming, I'm gonna hide. Yes, you do and he'll get you. So now it's fate to hide because if they get, she says, you just go right on by and don't you look at them. You just walk right by and they won't bother you. They didn't. I don't suppose I've ever seen any if I had, I guess I'd have died right there.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: We got found to mail one time. Somebody had lost it off in their wagon and we went and hid it in the corner of the fence where nobody could find it. So we come home and said, we found some mail, we hid it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Mother, where'd you put it? No, she says, what'd you do with it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: We hid it. She said, you get that and you take it back to the post office right away. And in the morning when you go to school. So that's somebody's mail and they wanted it.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Was there letters?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, there was letters. We hid them. We went to very big. We were looking after that mail, we hid them in the corner of the fence. Just you take them back to the post office. So we had to get them and get them back to the post office. It's funny how a kid would wanna do that. It'd be real helpful. It was protective. Mm -hmm. We couldn't read. We didn't know who's the worst. We couldn't read, but we'd hide it in the fence.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What made you and your husband decide to go to Canada? What did you decide to go to Canada?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, there wasn't no more places down here. Everything had been taken down here. And my brother went up to Canada and he found this land. And you could take it to Homestead. And he thought it'd be nice for us to go up there. And then you could buy Hudson Bay land for some, I think just a dollar and 80 cents an acre. He had to pay for that. So we took the homestead 160 acres and then we bought 160 acres of Hudson Bay land. It belongs to Hudson Bay, I guess. Well, I think that was a royalty for England. For the royalties for England. That had a dollar and 80 cents an acre. That belonged to England. Somebody wrote it. I don't know. They said it belonged to the royal family in England. So.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Down here all you could do was rent land.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, either buy it or rent it. It wasn't too high. It was high. It was cheap then. But look at it now. Six and $800 an acre.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Who wants it at that price and worth it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And then you have to pay such a big price to get the machinery to work it. You could buy a farm, now you could retire. My nephew bought a tractor of $30 ,000.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: One thing I'd rather have is $30 ,000, wouldn't you?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: But he had to, he had their land. He was running about, I guess, 15 or 16 hundred acres of land and he had to have it.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: He had to have it. That tractor's got a air conditioner, got a radio and everything.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Going back to, did you end up, when did you go off to Canada? Did you ever, you know, did you, were you behind the move? Did you want to do it too?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, I didn't. I didn't want to go. Johnny and my brother went on ahead of them, helped us with it. They chartered a car, you see, a railroad car and took our stuff up and we stayed here till they went up and built the house, house first. They built our house first, then my sister -in -law and I went up and took the children and then they built their house later. We all stayed darkly and built the house later, their house, a mile and a half from us. But they didn't stay long, they come back. We stayed, we went up in 12 and come back in 37. Come back to Pullman in 37. And we stayed there 10 years, then moved up here. It's been years since. My aunt went up on that train after their, me and got the house built, two kids with a whooping cough on the train.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They had their nerve.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Like two of their kids had whooping cough?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, none of mine. Mine.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Yours?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah. All of mine had it, we went and left here. They'd been exposed, they hadn't had it, they didn't have it yet, but they took it on that way. We got to a task and stayed probably stay overnight and kids would cough and they'd cough.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said, have your kids got the whooping cough?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, they took cold on the train or something, and they'd sure cough. We couldn't go downstairs to eat or nothing. We had to have everything set up too. They were just taking us then. We didn't allow them to go out amongst anybody. We made them stay in the room. We should have went on. That night, but we thought we'd just stay overnight in Potassa and it wouldn't be so hard, it's a hard trip. We had to stay the next night, about 10 o 'clock, the next night. We could have went on, but we didn't. But after the kids got the coughing, so we wished we hadn't went on. But we told them they must have caught cold on the train, I guess they did. They didn't come out of it.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Did you have it too?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I think so.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard up there? Especially at first, in Canada?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, you couldn't buy anything, Harley. You couldn't buy any fresh fruit or any fresh vegetables. We had to eat. Well, no, we couldn't get anything, Harley, there for the first two, three years. We just had to, we couldn't buy any clothing, Harley. I know I got... People send the meal order catalogs. Yeah, they send them, catalogs. For all their yardage and their clothes and hanging cinch. There's no use going to town like this because there's only one little store there. Send the Eaton's.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: So they just send the meal order, huh?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: The Simpson's. Mm.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes,

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: we was there a long time. We made money there and we spent it there too. I knew that five years without a crop. Every year you'd think now you're gonna get it. Well, last year was there. Our crop come up nice. Oh, it must have got a foot high and looked real nice. Well, down in Velma lived down in Carterson, Alberta. That's right at the foot of the mountain over there. Rockies. And we went down there. There's gonna have a Jubilee of some kind down there. And we went down to that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: South of Lethbridge, just over the line. Yeah, in by Lethbridge. The Waterton.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And we went, we stayed three days, wasn't it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Three or four days. Mm -hmm. Went back and the hot wind hit that wheat. And it'll just land over like, well, just dying. And here come the Russian Tistles right over the top of it. You couldn't tell there'd ever been any wheat ever sowed there.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What came over the top of it?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Russian Tistles. Them Russian Tistles up there. We never seen them before. They wasn't never that bad. But right then and there was, I said, I'm done, Charlie. I'm moving out. I'm not gonna stay here another time. Another year, I'm going. Well, what made us come with Melvin was a young man. I know he and Gladys was going to get married up there. And it, and it just wasn't anything there for him. And then we didn't have any feed for the stock. Nothing for yours. It didn't raise a thing. And we just thought we'd settle off the stock. And we was going to take them down there, British Columbia, down to where Belva was. And we got a pasture and everything to take them down. And then they passed a law that the Saskatchewan couldn't bring their stock over into Alberta. So we couldn't take them over there. That along after we, and we just, we got rid of all of them. We had to give them away and sold them as best we could. We thought, well, if we put loose now, we'd just come on down here. I'm glad we did. But up there, you're not sure of a crop yet.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You went five years in a row without a crop?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yeah, five years without a crop. I'll tell you. And there wasn't that much for stock to eat either. This year, bunch of grass. It didn't get, it wasn't a bunch of grass. It was just kind of a little short grass. And it'd cook, it'd get hot and dry. It'd dry up and there's nothing harder. Just, they had sloughs there, little water, they'd be green around that, down in around there. We just got out of there, had enough of it. So we just left everything, left them. They left two beds upstairs, curtains up the windows. And not only on the floors, of course I'd give it all away. And then just walked out. Kind of hard to do, you know, to back up a truck and take out just what out of the house you can do. Your truck will pick up at once. And take just what it'll hold up and go. Leave the rest there. Neighbors, I told the neighbors they could go in and get what they wanted. They did, I guess.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You sold the place when you left it, right? No?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: With the mortgage. We barged just to throw it in the bed and let them keep it. They could have it. Yeah, they could have it.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Then after we'd done that, they were satisfied. Then they come down here, was gonna garnish each other's wages down here, because they didn't get crops up there on that land. Well, we got hold of a lawyer and he wrote him a letter and I guess he says, they'll not budge. From the Dakota line in Saskatchewan. In Saskatchewan.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And it's awful dry there. Now you take it in south of us and up in there. They don't even have water. They just don't have water. We went back up, going up to our daughters and we had to go through the southern part of Alberta. Well, we was going to get a motel and there was no motel in that thing. It's getting dark. I guess he's getting cold out there.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: No, I didn't mean to.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How many ended up being in that capital?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I guess there's about 18 of us. And so we burned our oil stuff, cook on. And cold outside, oh brother, it was cold. I don't know how we ever got our clothes dry. But we did, we lived through some way, I don't know. I think there's, and we had some pigs that we bought, oh, about 175 pounds.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was it easy to break?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And take one of them about every two weeks. Take a pig every two weeks for meat for that bunch. And everything else. Well, I don't know why they come to our place. We didn't know them, but they did. They come right there and sit down on us. And no money, no place to go.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: What can you do but keep them?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How'd you sleep so that many people?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Well, I let them have that, Oliver's and his wife and baby have the bedroom, one bedroom. And then Skinner and his wife and kids slept on a Davenport we had in the living room. And then up, the boys, there's two Skinner boys and our little boy and my brother and his boy. Our bed set in the middle of the room, just a big like this, there's no petition upstairs. It was just floored. And our bed set in the middle. And then we put curtains around it. The girls slept on the other side, the boys on this side. We loved them. No, no, no.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: I don't know how we ever got. Boy, in the dead of winter, you didn't have many places to go except stay right in that house and get mad at each other. Oh yeah, we did go. We went up there when it was 30 below zero, we even stayed home. Oh, we'd get up, we'd be there at home and somebody come by. Come on, let's go with the neighbors. We're going over there and we asked women at Quilt and the men would play cards. And we'd say maybe one or two o 'clock in the morning we'd get home. Everything froze up at home. The fire went out. Had water and the dipper in the bucket. If you float in your water bucket, you couldn't get it up. I'd say every time I go out and I'm not going out again, I'm gonna stay home and I'm gonna keep this house warm. Take a long time to get warm. Once it got cold, you'd like to never get it warmed up. And beds would be so cold it'd be like there's water and it's damp. But next time somebody come on, here we go.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: And that's when we never, all through the summer we had ladies' aid. Once a month we'd all get together. Oh yeah, we had good, they're nice people up there. They're really nice. Oh yeah, we had a good time. So that's been served. None of us had anything. When I got there we had met you. I went to dance. We met 20 girls here, 25 girls there. Whole bunch of us were just playing. We'd be good for dance. We'd be there in the morning. Seven o 'clock when we'd be there. Because we'd have to wait, see.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Your dad said it was rose?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Because if it's a cold it might be a lizard come in or all of you. We'd have to wait a long time. It would blow the road full and we'd get lost. So we'd have to wait till daylight to see where it goes. Yeah, they'd have to wait till daylight to get home. And then sometimes it's so hazy you couldn't tell where you're going. Oh yeah. You people would freeze to death up there. But it even frees the horse's ears off. Her husband had a pony. I don't know how, but it did throw stairs off up there. No, it wasn't. It was a little coat.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: It was a little coat that throws the ears off, wasn't it? Did they just fall off?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, come clear off. It's not ours.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: It wasn't ours. We didn't have no coat.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't Toots have a coat that froze its ears?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: Yes, she did.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: She must have had it off. She did. She had it outside and it froze its ears off.

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
words: They went, oh man. Oh, it was cold. And 30 below zero, and his wife was looking for a baby. They lived down south and they had 10 miles. They had about 12 miles to go, or 14. And with the team. Well, in the wintertime we went across this big lake. It froze over and just neared, you know. Well, she got right in the middle of this big lake. She had her baby in the sleighs. In that cold, oh, right in that old sleighs and old dirty comforts and everything. And he run his team the rest of the way into town. And they got him in there and he said, they come out and got her. I don't know how they got her the baby in there, but they did some way in the hospital. Oh, that land in there. Next way.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But now, she got left out in the lake?

speaker: Ada Hill Crow or Velma Gertz
